full_response:
Step 1   Born-rigid (constant proper length) uniformly accelerated motion  
A rigid rocket that is accelerating in flat space–time is described by the Rindler family of world-lines.  
In Minkowski coordinates (c=1)

x(τ) = ξ cosh(τ/ξ),  t(τ) = ξ sinh(τ/ξ)             (1)

where  

ξ = constant = “Rindler position’’ of the given point .

For every world-line of the form (1) the magnitude of the 4-acceleration is

α = 1/ξ            (2)

Thus the proper acceleration is inversely proportional to the distance ξ from the Rindler horizon (x = 0).

Step 2   Fix the data for the top of the rocket  
At the top we (you) measure

α_top = 1 g ≃ 1 ly / yr² .                             (3)

From (2)

ξ_top = 1/α_top = 1 yr .                               (4)

Step 3   Relate top and bottom through the proper length of the rocket  
For Born rigidity the proper length L measured on a surface of simultaneity η = const (equivalently t/x = tanh η = const) is simply the difference of the ξ–coordinates:

L = ξ_top − ξ_bottom .                                (5)

Given in the problem:

L = 0.99 ly = 0.99 yr (since c = 1).                  (6)

Therefore

ξ_bottom = ξ_top − L = 1 yr − 0.99 yr = 0.01 yr .     (7)

Step 4   Proper acceleration at the bottom  
Insert (7) into (2):

α_bottom = 1/ξ_bottom = 1 / (0.01 yr) = 100 yr⁻¹ .    (8)

Step 5   Express in familiar units (multiples of g)  
Because 1 yr⁻¹ = 1 ly / yr², comparison with (3) shows

α_bottom = 100 ly / yr² = 100 g .                     (9)

\boxed{\alpha_{\text{bottom}} = 100\,g \; \; (\text{approximately } 100\;\text{ly/yr}^2)}  

Confidence: 86%
